summary:
Teppe Sialk, located near modern Kashan on the central Iranian plateau, is one of the oldest continuously occupied settlements in Iran, with its earliest occupation layers dating to approximately 6000 BCE. The site sits at the edge of a seasonal river and near ancient copper ore deposits, a combination that likely made it an attractive location for early farmers and metalworkers. The lowest stratigraphic levels contain evidence of mudbrick architecture, painted pottery, and simple copper tools.

Teppe Sialk's early occupation represents the penetration of Neolithic agricultural communities into the Iranian plateau. The painted ceramics of the earliest levels show stylistic connections to contemporary cultures in Mesopotamia and the Zagros foothills, indicating that Sialk was part of a broader network of cultural interaction. The site would continue to be occupied through the Chalcolithic and into the Bronze Age, eventually becoming a center of significant metalworking and artistic production.

read_more_links.title: Prehistory of the Near East – Wikipedia
